===== slide 15 =====
Partial vs total correctness
when the precondition is met,
executing the command
establishes the postcondition

partial

{P} c {Q}

total

when the precondition is met,
executing the command terminates
and establishes the postcondition
total correctness = partial correctness + termination

Choose a an arithmetic expression on program

• t is bounded below
• therefore the loop cannot execute infinitely many
times

===== slide 29 =====
Correctness
Th. Any derivable HL triple is valid
Proof. By induction on the derivation tree.
Remember the proof for composition of commands

{P} c1 {R} {R} c2 {Q}
{P} c1; c2 {Q}
[[c1; c2]]P = [[c2]]([[c1]]P) ⊆ [[c2]]R ⊆ Q

===== slide 30 =====
Floyd’s axiom soundness
{P} x := a { ∃x′ . P[x′/x] ∧ x = a[x′/x]}

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

￼

Let us take δ ∈ [[x := a]]P,
we need to show that δ ⊧ ∃x′ . P[x′/x] ∧ x = a[x′/x]
It must be the case that δ = σ[v/x] for some σ ⊧ P such that v = [[a]]σ
Let x′ = σ(x). Then δ(x) = v and
[[a[x′/x]]]δ =[[a[x′/x]]]σ[v/x] = [[a[x′/x]]]σ = [[a]]σ = v.
Therefore, δ ⊧ (x = a[x′/x]).
Moreover, [[P[x′/x]]]δ = [[P[x′/x]]]σ[v/x] = [[P[x′/x]]]σ = [[P]]σ = true
Therefore, δ ⊧ P[x′/x].
From which we conclude δ ⊧ ∃x′ . P[x′/x] ∧ x = a[x′/x]

===== slide 31 =====
Conditional rule: correctness
{P ∧ b} c1 {Q} {P ∧ ¬b} c2 {Q}
{P} if b then c1 else c2 {Q}
We assume the validity of the premises:
[[c1]](P ∧ b) ⊆ Q and [[c2]](P ∧ ¬b) ⊆ Q
Therefore:
[[if b then c1 else c2]]P = [[b?; c1 + ¬b?; c2]]P
= [[c1]]([[b]]P) ∪[[c2]]([[ ¬b]]P)
= [[c1]](P ∧ b) ∪ [[c2]](P ∧ ¬b) ⊆ Q ∪ Q = Q

Incompleteness II

===== slide 35 =====
Relative completeness I
Relative completeness: suppose we can consult an oracle to
check if an assertion P ⇒ P′ is valid or not, then HL is complete

￼

In other words, we separate concerns about programs and
reasoning about them from concerns to do with arithmetic and
the incompleteness of any proof system for it

===== slide 36 =====
Dijkstra’s weakest precondition
Given a command c and a postcondition Q a weakest liberal
precondition is a predicate P such that for any precondition R

{R} c {Q} i R ⇒ P
i.e., P is the least restrictive requirement that guarantees that
Q holds after executing c (if it terminates)

ff

ij

Typically, it is denoted by wlp(c, Q) ≜ {σ ∈ Σ ∣ [[c]]{σ} ⊆ Q}

===== slide 37 =====
Adjoints
P ⇒ wlp(c, Q)
i

ff

[[c]]P ⊆ Q

===== slide 38 =====
(Relative) Completeness
for any postcondition Q expressible in the logic
and for any command c, the precondition
wlp(c, Q) is also expressible in the logic

Th. If the logic language is expressive enough, then any valid HL
triple can be derived.
Proof. Suppose {P} c {Q} is valid (with P and Q expressible).
By structural induction on c we can build an assertion R that is
equivalent to wlp(c, Q) and such that {R} c {Q} is derivable.
By applying the consequence rule we derive {P} c {Q}.
